one_line_takeaway: RAGOrigin reframes RAG poisoning from prevention to forensics, using a two-stage scope-narrowing plus tri-signal (retrieval + semantic + generation) unsupervised-clustering method to trace the exact poisoned texts behind a bad answer with DACC ~0.98-1.00, FPR/FNR <=0.03, and near-zero residual attack success after removal -- notably surviving denial-of-service attacks that break the prior RAGForensics baseline.
key_finding: RAGOrigin achieves near-perfect attribution and generalizes where baselines collapse. On NQ and HotpotQA it reaches DACC 0.97-1.00 with FPR <=0.03 and FNR <=0.01 across all 9 attacks. The strongest prior work, RAGForensics, matches RAGOrigin on answer-manipulation attacks (DACC ~0.95-0.99) but collapses on denial-of-service attacks (e.g. HotpotQA: DACC 0.22 Jamming, 0.27 Phantom; FNR up to 0.87) because it relies on explicit semantic overlap between poisoned text and output. Practically decisive: after removing the texts RAGOrigin flags, attack success rate drops to 0.00-0.01 across all attacks and all 5 datasets, whereas removing RAGForensics' flags leaves DoS ASR at 0.55-0.97. RAGOrigin stays robust under all 3 adaptive attacks (e.g. DACC >=0.98, FPR/FNR <=0.03 vs benign-text perturbation on NQ), scales to the 16.7M-text database (DACC 0.98-1.00), and holds on open-ended ELI5 (DACC 1.00, FPR/FNR <=0.01). It is cheap: <=2.11s per misgeneration event on the 8.5M-text MS-MARCO DB (comparable to the ~1.3s RAG answer time) and <=$0.0004 per event with GPT-4o-mini.
core_contribution: RAGOrigin, a black-box POST-ATTACK responsibility attribution (forensics) framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Unlike defenses that try to PREVENT poisoning, RAGOrigin answers 'who taught the lie?': given a reported misgeneration event (a user question plus the incorrect response), it identifies WHICH texts in the knowledge database are responsible. It works in two stages. (1) Adaptive scope construction: rank all DB texts by embedding similarity to the question, then iteratively test size-K segments as LLM context until at least half of the tested segments no longer reproduce the incorrect response, bounding the search to a few dozen candidate texts instead of millions. (2) Responsibility measurement: each candidate gets a score combining three z-score-normalized signals -- embedding similarity ES(u) (retriever ranking), semantic correlation SC(u) (avg log-prob a proxy LLM assigns to the question given the text as context), and generation influence GC(u) (avg log-prob the proxy LLM assigns to the incorrect response given the text). The three are averaged, and K-means (k=2) separates poisoned from benign texts with no labeled data or manual threshold. The design is grounded in two necessary conditions for a successful RAG poisoning attack (high retrieval similarity AND high influence on generation), so it is attack-agnostic.
threat_model: Attacker injects a small number of crafted poisoned texts (default M=5 per target question) into the RAG knowledge database to force an attacker-chosen target answer for a target question. Two attacker settings: white-box (access to retriever parameters, e.g. the public UAE-Large-V1 retriever used by NVIDIA ChatRTX, but not the DB or LLM) and black-box (no access to any component). Attacks targeting/manipulating the retriever itself are explicitly out of scope (retrievers are often closed-source). The DEFENDER/forensics system is the service provider: it has access to the knowledge database and the reported misgeneration event (question + incorrect response) but NO access to retriever/LLM gradients or parameters, and no prior knowledge of the attack strategy or the number of poisoned texts. Users are assumed to honestly report misgenerations (a common assumption, supported by built-in feedback buttons in deployed LLM apps). A strong adaptive attacker with full knowledge of RAGOrigin's design is also considered.
limitations: Scope is deliberately narrow: it only performs attribution AFTER a misgeneration is reported, and only for misgenerations CAUSED BY poisoning -- LLM-inherent errors are handled by a separate query-and-compare heuristic, not the core method. It requires the misgeneration event (question + incorrect response) as input and assumes users honestly report such events; it does not address correct answers or malicious/false reports. Attacks that target the retriever itself are excluded by assumption. The three signals are computed with a proxy LLM (Llama3.1-8B by default); performance under weaker/mismatched proxies is only partially explored (Table 11/12 argue robustness). The adaptive-attack evaluation, while strong, is designed by the authors, so residual attack strategies that jointly evade all three signals remain an open question the authors themselves acknowledge raising the bar for rather than eliminating.

datasets_used: Large-scale knowledge database of 16.7 million texts (Section 7)
quantitative_results: RAGOrigin on NQ: DACC 0.98-1.00, FPR <=0.03, FNR 0.00 across all 9 attacks
RAGOrigin on HotpotQA: DACC 0.97-1.00, FPR <=0.03, FNR 0.00 across all 9 attacks
After removing RAGOrigin-flagged texts, post-removal ASR = 0.00-0.01 across all 9 attacks on all 5 datasets
RAGForensics collapses on DoS attacks on HotpotQA: DACC 0.22 (Jamming), 0.45 (BadRAG), 0.27 (Phantom), 0.58 (AgentPoison); FNR up to 0.87
RAGForensics leaves residual DoS ASR high after removal, e.g. NQ 0.68-0.93, MS-MARCO 0.63-0.97, BoolQ 0.55-0.97
Adaptive benign-text perturbation on NQ: RAGOrigin DACC >=0.98, FPR/FNR <=0.03
ELI5 open-ended: DACC 1.00, FPR/FNR <=0.01 across all attacks
Runtime <=2.11s per misgeneration event on MS-MARCO (>8.5M texts); RAG answer time 1.29-1.39s across datasets
Monetary cost <=$0.0004 per misgeneration event using GPT-4o-mini
Attack setup: M=5 poisoned texts injected per target question; PoisonedRAG per-question poisoning rate <0.0002%
Baseline ASR without any defense (NQ): 0.69 (HijackRAG) up to 0.99 (Phantom)
baselines_compared: PFDNN (DNN poison forensics, adapted with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 + K-means) [29]
